body_txt: In fiction, cohabitation (e.g. dormitories, apartments) is a fruitful ground for romantic and/or sexual tension (requited or no). Not only do situations like shared bathrooms, skimpy loungewear, late nights, and house parties set up Ship Tease , the living situation also allows the characters to get to know each other in their off time. For example, a domineering character's roommate might learn that they are in fact a great baker or a fan of romantic comedies . Living together can also provide several relationship-developing plots that a non-roommate couple might have to come up with excuses to use: the characters can cook together, go grocery shopping, or tend to one another during the Sick Episode . It can also provide Plausible Deniability for their closeness — no , their weekly movie nights aren't dates... until they are. The Relationship Upgrade might be preceded by a desire not to mess with the compatible living arrangement . If the characters are adults who then get a Relationship Upgrade , they won't even need to go through the whole moving-in together part, and the writers can decide to have the characters keep the place (and save having to come up with a whole new setting). This might be exploited by someone who agrees to a roommate situation with ulterior motives. This trope can be done with any combination of gender and sexual orientation. In the case of opposite-sex roommates, living together was once strictly only for family members, married couples, or very serious relationships, and the characters may have to tangle with that assumption : the Disposable Fiancé , for example, might visit his partner's place, meet her male roommate, and have to accept a He Is Not My Boyfriend explanation while hoping he won't get dumped for said roommate. Falling for your roommate in a non-coed environment such as Boarding School might be used as an explanation for Situational Sexuality ; alternatively, being around one or more people of the same gender during and after puberty might serve as a Closet Key for a younger character. From Roommates to Romance is justifiable for Magical Girlfriends because they tend not to have anywhere else to stay and Unwanted Harems because Pretty Freeloaders make for easier contact. Might feature in the romance subplot of a Roommate Com . Contrast Roommate Drama , for when roommates clash with each other (though they can certainly overlap in cases of Belligerent Sexual Tension ). Compare There Is Only One Bed , for when characters are forced to share a bed. Subtrope of Romance Ensues , a Plot Device that forces characters to spend time alone together, leading to the development of romantic/sexual tension.
